posts.title: Agents that recover from failure
posts.hook: The agent that fails gracefully will outperform the agent that never fails.
posts.body:
The agent that fails gracefully will outperform the agent that never fails.

I run 6 automated agents in production right now. Content generation, CRM enrichment, Reddit scouting, SEO monitoring, data sync, blog publishing.

The ones that work best aren't the ones I spent the most time prompt engineering. They're the ones where I spent the most time on the failure paths.

What happens when the API times out? When the LLM hallucinates a field name? When the data source returns empty? When the cron fires but the dev server isn't running?

Every one of those has bitten me. My enrichment pipeline died for 11 days because a dev server stopped and nothing checked for it.

The fix wasn't a better model. It was a launchd plist with KeepAlive set to true and a health check that pings the endpoint before writing.

Most people building agents are optimizing the happy path. Better prompts, bigger context windows, fancier orchestration.

The builders shipping reliable agents are obsessing over the sad path. Retry logic, fallback defaults, dead letter queues, alerting when something silently stops.

Reliability isn't a model problem. It's an engineering problem. And engineering problems have engineering solutions.
posts.cta: What's the dumbest failure that took down one of your automations?

remixes.label: war-stories-from-prod
remixes.hook: My best agent went down at 2am because a Reddit API returned HTML instead of JSON. That failure taught me more than any prompt engineering course.
remixes.body:
My best agent went down at 2am because a Reddit API returned HTML instead of JSON. That failure taught me more than any prompt engineering course.

I've been running automated agents in production for months. Six of them, doing everything from content generation to CRM enrichment to SEO monitoring.

 about production agents. The prompt is maybe 20% of the work. The other 80% is:

Retry logic when APIs return garbage.
Graceful degradation when a dependency is down.
Logging that actually tells you what happened at 2am.
Timeout handling so one stuck call doesn't cascade.
Idempotency so a retry doesn't double-post your content.

I learned this the hard way. My Reddit scouting agent would silently fail whenever Reddit rate-limited it. No error. No log. Just... nothing. For three days I thought Reddit was quiet. It wasn't.

Now every agent I build starts with the failure paths. What happens when this breaks? What does recovery look like? How do I know it broke?

The agents that survive production aren't clever. They're resilient. They catch exceptions, log context, back off, retry, and alert me when something is genuinely wrong.

Prompt engineering gets the demos. Error handling gets the uptime.
remixes.cta: What's the most silent failure you've caught in an automation? The ones that don't throw errors are the scariest.

remixes.label: defensive-engineering-mindset
remixes.hook: Stop optimizing your agent prompts. Start optimizing your agent fallbacks.
remixes.body:
Stop optimizing your agent prompts. Start optimizing your agent fallbacks.

I see people spending weeks on prompt engineering, tweaking temperature, adding few-shot examples, testing different system prompts. That work matters. But it's not what separates a demo from a production system.

I run 6 agents in production. The difference between the reliable ones and the flaky ones has nothing to do with the prompts. It comes down to three things:

1. Circuit breakers. If an API fails 3 times in a row, stop calling it. Don't burn credits on a dead endpoint.

2. State checkpoints. If your agent is doing a 5-step process and step 3 fails, can it resume from step 3? Or does it start over and duplicate steps 1 and 2?

3. Observable failures. Every failure should leave a breadcrumb. Not just a stack trace. Context. What was the input? What was the agent trying to do? What was the state of the world?

Most agent frameworks give you zero help with any of this. They're built for the happy path. You need to build the sad path yourself.

The irony is that defensive engineering makes your agents look smarter. They're not. They just recover faster than they break.
remixes.cta: Before you ship an agent, ask: what happens when this fails at 3am and nobody is watching?

tones.advisor:
If you're running agents in production, I'd recommend spending 60% of your development time on failure paths. Not prompt engineering. Not architecture. Failure paths.

I run 6 automated agents right now. Content generation, CRM enrichment, Reddit scouting, SEO monitoring, data sync, blog publishing. The pattern is clear. The reliable ones are the ones where I invested in retry logic, graceful degradation, and meaningful logging.

Here's what I recommend for anyone deploying agents beyond a demo:

First, map every external dependency and define what happens when each one is unavailable. Second, implement structured logging with enough context to diagnose problems without reproducing them. Third, build idempotency into every write operation so retries don't create duplicates.

The API times out. The model returns malformed JSON. The upstream data changes shape. These aren't edge cases. They're Tuesday.

The teams I advise that ship reliable agents all share one trait. They treat failure handling as a first-class feature, not a cleanup task after launch. The prompt gets the agent working. The failure handling keeps it working.

Hot take: your agent doesn't need a better prompt. It needs better error handling. And you're ignoring that because error handling isn't fun to demo on Twitter.

I run 6 agents in production. The ones that actually work? They're not the clever ones. They're the ones where I was paranoid enough to ask "what happens when this breaks" before shipping.

The AI agent community has a massive blind spot. Everyone shares their architectures, their prompt chains, their multi-step reasoning flows. Nobody shares their retry logic. Nobody posts about their circuit breakers. Nobody brags about graceful degradation.

Because it's boring. But boring is what keeps systems running.

Here's : if your agent can't handle a timeout, a malformed response, or a rate limit without human intervention, it's not an agent. It's a script with a good demo.

I've seen agents that cost $50K to build go down because nobody handled a 429 response code. That's not an architecture problem. That's a culture problem. We've been so obsessed with making agents smarter that we forgot to make them sturdy.

The agent that fails gracefully at 3am will always beat the one that needs you to restart it.

posts.title: Outbound is broken because ops is missing
posts.hook: Outbound automation doesn't fail because of bad copy. It fails because nobody built the data layer underneath it.
posts.body:
Outbound automation doesn't fail because of bad copy. It fails because nobody built the data layer underneath it.

I sent 200+ cold emails a day as an SDR. Primary domain. No warmup tools. SalesLoft sequences with manual personalization because we didn't have anything else.

The emails that worked had one thing in common. I actually knew something real about the account before I wrote them.

Now I watch teams throw AI at outbound and wonder why reply rates are worse than 2019. The sequences are technically better. The personalization tokens are filled. The send infrastructure is warmed.

But the enrichment is surface level. Job title, company size, maybe a recent funding round pulled from a stale database.

The play that works now: build a research layer that runs before the sequence touches a single contact. Technographic signals from job postings. Hiring patterns that reveal pain. Tool stack changes that create timing.

Clay can do this in a single table. Pull the job board, extract tech mentions, score against your ICP, route to the right sequence based on what you actually found.

The automation isn't the hard part. The intelligence feeding it is.
posts.cta: If your enrichment layer takes less than 30 seconds per account, it's probably not enriching anything.

remixes.label: data-layer-first
remixes.hook: Your outbound sequences aren't underperforming because of the copy. They're underperforming because your data is shallow.
remixes.body:
Your outbound sequences aren't underperforming because of the copy. They're underperforming because your data is shallow.

I spent two years as an SDR sending cold emails. Primary domain. No warmup tools. Manual personalization because we didn't have enrichment software.

The emails that booked meetings all had one thing in common. I knew something real about the account before I wrote the first line. Not "congrats on the funding round" real. Actually real. What tools they used. What their team structure looked like. What problem they were probably dealing with based on their hiring patterns.

That took 15-20 minutes per account. It didn't scale. But it worked.

Now everyone's trying to automate outbound and they're automating the wrong layer. They're plugging AI into the copy generation step and feeding it the same thin data every SDR has access to. Company name, title, industry, maybe a recent press mention.

The leverage isn't in writing emails faster. It's in building a richer data layer so the emails have something real to say.

Enrichment that actually works looks like this: technographic data, hiring signals, org chart mapping, content the prospect has published, pain points inferred from their stack.

That takes infrastructure. Not a better prompt template.
remixes.cta: If your enrichment takes 5 seconds per account, you're just reformatting firmographics and calling it personalization.

remixes.label: sdr-to-engineer-pipeline
remixes.hook: I went from SDR to GTM engineer because I got tired of doing enrichment by hand. The data problem behind outbound is an engineering problem.
remixes.body:
I went from SDR to GTM engineer because I got tired of doing enrichment by hand. The data problem behind outbound is an engineering problem.

When I was sending 200+ cold emails a day, I noticed something. The reps who booked the most meetings weren't better writers. They were better researchers. They found angles that generic templates couldn't produce.

But research doesn't scale manually. So most teams skip it. They buy a list, write three templates, set up a sequence, and wonder why reply rates are under 2%.

The missing layer is ops infrastructure. Specifically:

Account scoring that goes beyond firmographics. Who's actually in-market, not just in your ICP?

Enrichment pipelines that pull technographic signals, hiring data, and content signals into one place before a single email gets written.

Data hygiene automation that catches bad emails, stale contacts, and duplicate records before they tank your domain reputation.

This isn't a sales problem. It's a systems problem. And the people best equipped to solve it aren't copywriters or sales managers. They're engineers who understand data pipelines.

The outbound teams that win in 2026 will have someone who can build these layers. Not just someone who can write a good subject line.
remixes.cta: Next time outbound underperforms, look at the data layer before you rewrite the copy.

If your outbound isn't converting, I'd recommend looking past the copy and into the data infrastructure underneath it.

Having sent 200+ cold emails a day as an SDR on a primary domain with no warmup tools, I can tell you what separated the emails that worked from the ones that didn't. It wasn't the subject line or the CTA. It was whether I actually knew something real about the account before writing.

Here's what I recommend for teams serious about outbound performance:

Build an enrichment layer that takes at least 30 seconds per account. If it's faster than that, you're pulling surface-level firmographics and calling it research.

Stack multiple data sources. Technographic signals, hiring patterns, published content, org structure. Each layer compounds.

Separate the data pipeline from the sequence tool. SalesLoft and Outreach are delivery mechanisms, not intelligence platforms. Feed them better inputs.

The teams I've seen succeed at scale treat their data layer as a product. They iterate on it, measure its impact on reply rates, and invest engineering time into it. The teams that struggle keep rewriting email templates hoping for a different result.

Outbound is a data problem wearing a copywriting mask.

Outbound isn't broken because of bad copy. Outbound is broken because the entire industry decided that "personalization" means dropping a first name and company name into a template.

I sent 200+ cold emails a day as an SDR. Primary domain. No warmup. And the emails that actually booked meetings had one thing in common. I knew something real about the account. Not LinkedIn-bio real. Actually real.

But nobody wants to hear that. Because real enrichment is slow, expensive, and hard to automate. So instead we get "AI-powered personalization" that reads a company's About page and generates three sentences of corporate astrology.

"I noticed [Company] is focused on innovation in the [Industry] space." Wow. Groundbreaking stuff.

The dirty secret of outbound automation: most tools are optimizing the wrong step. They're making it faster to send bad emails. The bottleneck was never writing speed. It was intel quality.

If your enrichment layer takes less than 30 seconds per account, you're not enriching. You're decorating. And your prospects can smell the difference.

The fix isn't a better prompt. It's a better data pipeline. But that requires engineering work, not another SaaS subscription.

posts.title: One person GTM stack
posts.hook: A solo operator with Claude Code, Clay, and a Next.js site can now do what a 4-person GTM team did 18 months ago.
posts.body:
A solo operator with Claude Code, Clay, and a Next.js site can now do what a 4-person GTM team did 18 months ago.

Not theoretically. I'm living it.

My current stack as a one-person GTM engineering consultancy:

✅ Clay for enrichment and ICP scoring
✅ Claude Code for building automations, content pipelines, and landing pages
✅ Next.js monorepo with 3 sites, a mission control app, and a CRM integration
✅ Cron jobs for daily content, SEO monitoring, Reddit scouting, and data sync
✅ Attio for CRM with custom objects and automated deal flow
✅ Typefully for scheduled social distribution

All of it runs on a single Mac Mini. No team. No VAs. No agency.

The insight people miss about the solo builder movement isn't that tools got cheaper. It's that the integration layer got accessible. Claude Code can wire together APIs that used to require a full-stack engineer and a week of sprint planning.

I generate prospect landing pages, run ABM campaigns, publish SEO content, and manage pipeline from the same terminal.

This isn't hustle culture. It's leverage. The gap between "I could build that" and "I shipped that" collapsed when AI agents started writing the glue code.
posts.cta: The new competitive advantage isn't headcount. It's how fast one person can ship a system.

inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo developer building and shipping multiple products like Nomad List and Remote OK without funding. He emphasizes the power of building in public, iterating based on user feedback, and achieving profitability through bootstrapping. His post inspires others to start small and ship fast.

remixes.label: stack-teardown
remixes.hook: I replaced a 4-person GTM team with a monorepo, three AI models, and a lot of cron jobs. Here's the actual stack.
remixes.body:
I replaced a 4-person GTM team with a monorepo, three AI models, and a lot of cron jobs. Here's the actual stack.

Not a thought experiment. This is what I'm running right now as a solo GTM engineering consultancy.

The infrastructure:
A Next.js monorepo hosting 3 websites, a mission control dashboard, and CRM integrations. All deployed on Cloudflare.

The AI layer:
Claude Opus for content creation and complex analysis. Claude Haiku for fast enrichment and classification. Local Qwen 2.5 for high-frequency crons that would burn through API credits.

The automation:
Claude Code builds the systems. Not just code. Full automation pipelines, landing pages, content workflows, data sync.

The GTM ops:
Clay handles enrichment and ICP scoring. Attio for CRM. PostHog for analytics. Custom scripts for Reddit scouting, SEO monitoring, and prospect research.

Total headcount: one.

The thing that makes this possible isn't any single tool. It's that these tools now compose. You can chain an enrichment pipeline into a scoring model into a personalized outreach sequence. And one person can build and maintain that chain.

18 months ago this stack would have required a developer, a RevOps person, a content marketer, and a data analyst. The roles didn't disappear. They compressed into one person who can build systems.
remixes.cta: What's the smallest team you've seen run a full GTM motion? I'm curious where the floor is.

remixes.label: leverage-not-hustle
remixes.hook: The one-person GTM stack isn't about working harder. It's about building systems that compound while you sleep.
remixes.body:
The one-person GTM stack isn't about working harder. It's about building systems that compound while you sleep.

I run a solo GTM engineering consultancy. Every night at midnight, a cron job generates a blog post. Another one syncs my Reddit scout data. Another enriches new CRM contacts. Another monitors keyword rankings.

I didn't hire anyone to do this. I built systems.

The mental model shift that made this work: stop thinking in tasks, start thinking in pipelines. Every manual thing I did more than twice became a candidate for automation.

Week 1: I'm manually researching accounts in Clay.
Week 2: I build a Clay table that auto-enriches based on ICP criteria.
Week 3: That table feeds into Attio with scoring.
Week 4: Scored accounts trigger personalized landing pages.

Each layer compounds on the last one. That's the real advantage. Not speed. Compounding.

The tools that make this possible right now: Claude Code for building the automations. Clay for enrichment. Next.js for the web layer. Cloudflare for deployment. PostHog for knowing what's working.

But the tools change every 6 months. What doesn't change is the pattern. Build a system. Let it run. Build the next one on top of it.

That's how one person does what a team used to.
remixes.cta: What's one repetitive GTM task you're still doing manually that should be a pipeline?

For anyone considering the solo operator path, here's what I'd recommend based on running this stack for months.

The core of a one-person GTM operation is a composable stack where each layer feeds the next. Mine looks like this:

Clay for enrichment and ICP scoring. This is your intelligence layer. Without rich data, everything downstream suffers.

Claude Code for building automations, content pipelines, and landing pages. This is your engineering layer. It lets one person build what used to require a developer and a RevOps person.

A Next.js monorepo with 3 sites, a mission control dashboard, and CRM integration. This is your delivery layer.

Three AI models segmented by cost and capability. Opus for quality-sensitive work. Haiku for fast classification. Local Qwen for high-frequency tasks.

The key recommendation: don't try to adopt this stack all at once. Start with one pipeline end to end. Enrichment to scoring to outreach. Get that working reliably, then layer on content automation, then monitoring.

The competitive advantage isn't the tools. It's the ability to compose them into systems that run without you. Headcount is no longer the scaling lever. Systems are.

A solo operator with the right stack can now outperform a 4-person GTM team. And most GTM leaders don't want to hear it because it threatens their org chart.

I'm not theorizing. I'm living it. One-person consultancy. Three websites. Six automated agents. CRM enrichment, content generation, SEO monitoring, prospect research. All running on cron jobs and a Mac Mini.

The tools: Claude Code, Clay, Next.js, Attio, PostHog. Total software cost less than one junior hire's monthly salary.

Here's what nobody in GTM leadership wants to admit. Half the team meetings, Slack threads, and alignment sessions exist because the stack is fragmented across people who each own one piece. One person with a composable stack doesn't need alignment meetings. The data flows through a pipeline, not through a Slack channel.

Is this sustainable forever? Probably not. There's a ceiling. But that ceiling is way higher than most people think. And it's rising every month as tools get better.

The uncomfortable question for GTM leaders: if one engineer can build and run this, what exactly is your 8-person ops team doing that a well-architected system can't?

posts.title: Multi-agent is a deployment problem
posts.hook: Everyone's talking about multi-agent architectures. Almost nobody is talking about multi-agent ops.
posts.body:
Everyone's talking about multi-agent architectures. Almost nobody is talking about multi-agent ops.

I run multiple Claude-powered agents across different tasks. Content generation on Opus. Fast enrichment on Haiku. Local Qwen for high-frequency crons that would burn through API credits.

The architecture part was straightforward. The ops part nearly killed me.

Problems nobody warns you about:

🔧 Context handoffs between agents lose information unless you serialize state explicitly
🔧 Cron-triggered agents need health checks or they silently die for days
🔧 Model routing decisions compound. Sending the wrong task to the wrong model wastes money and produces garbage
🔧 Logging across agents is a mess unless you centralize it from day one
🔧 Rate limits hit differently when 4 agents are calling the same API

The agent framework discourse is focused on orchestration patterns. ReAct vs. plan-and-execute vs. tree of thought.

In production, the bottleneck is never the reasoning pattern. It's the plumbing. Process management, error recovery, credential rotation, output validation.

If you're building agents for a demo, optimize the prompt. If you're building agents for production, optimize the infrastructure around the prompt.
posts.cta: Ship one agent to production before designing your multi-agent framework. The lessons are in the ops.

inspired_by.author: Swyx
inspired_by.summary: Swyx discusses the evolution of AI agents, highlighting how multi-agent systems are transforming workflows from simple automation to complex decision-making architectures. He shares insights on integrating developer tools for scalable AI infrastructure. Emphasizes practical challenges in reliability and orchestration.

remixes.label: ops-horror-stories
remixes.hook: I built a multi-agent system in a weekend. I've spent three months keeping it alive.
remixes.body:
I built a multi-agent system in a weekend. I've spent three months keeping it alive.

The architecture was the easy part. Content agent on Opus. Enrichment agent on Haiku. High-frequency tasks on local Qwen. Clean separation of concerns. Elegant design.

Then I deployed it.

Week 1: The content agent and the data sync agent both try to write to the same file. Silent corruption.

Week 3: Local Qwen goes down because an OS update changed a Python path. Three crons fail silently for five days.

Week 5: The blog publishing agent stops working. Not because of a code bug. Because the dev server that connects to the database got killed by launchd. No error anywhere.

Week 8: I realize I have no way to know if an agent ran successfully unless I manually check the output. So I build a monitoring layer. That takes longer than building the agents.

This is the reality of multi-agent ops that Twitter threads don't cover. The architecture diagram fits on one slide. The operational runbook is a living document that grows every week.

Every agent framework I see focuses on orchestration. How to chain calls, how to pass context, how to handle tool use. None of them focus on: How do you know it's still working? What happens when a dependency dies? How do you debug a failure from 6 hours ago?
remixes.cta: If you're building multi-agent systems, start with one agent in production. Solve the ops problems there first. Then scale.

remixes.label: cost-tier-strategy
remixes.hook: Running every agent on GPT-4 or Claude Opus is like running every database query on your primary replica. You need a tiering strategy.
remixes.body:
Running every agent on GPT-4 or Claude Opus is like running every database query on your primary replica. You need a tiering strategy.

I run multiple agents across three different models. Not because I love complexity. Because the economics forced it.

Claude Opus for content generation and complex analysis. Quality matters here. The cost is worth it.

Claude Haiku for fast enrichment, classification, and simple transformations. 95% as good for these tasks at a fraction of the cost.

Local Qwen 2.5 (14B) for high-frequency crons. These run every hour. At API prices, that's hundreds per month. Locally, it's just electricity.

The tiering decision comes down to three variables: quality sensitivity, frequency, and latency requirements.

High quality sensitivity, low frequency? Use the best model you can afford.
Low quality sensitivity, high frequency? Use the cheapest model that meets the threshold.
Latency-critical? Local models eliminate network round trips.

Most multi-agent discussions assume homogeneous model deployment. Every agent gets the same model. That's the default because it's simple. But it's expensive and wasteful.

The real skill in multi-agent ops isn't prompt engineering. It's knowing which model to route each task to. Get this wrong and you're either burning money or shipping garbage.
remixes.cta: What model tiering strategy are you running? Or are you still defaulting everything to one model?

tones.builder:
Everyone's talking about multi-agent architectures. Almost nobody is talking about multi-agent ops.

I run multiple Claude-powered agents across different tasks. Content generation on Opus. Fast enrichment on Haiku. Local Qwen for high-frequency crons that would burn through API credits.

The architecture part was straightforward. The ops part nearly killed me.

Problems nobody warns you about:

🔧 Context handoffs between agents lose information unless you serialize state explicitly
🔧 Cron-triggered agents need health checks or they silently die for days
🔧 Model routing decisions compound. Sending the wrong task to the wrong model wastes money and produces garbage
🔧 Logging across agents is a mess unless you centralize it from day one
🔧 Rate limits hit differently when 4 agents are calling the same API

The agent framework discourse is focused on orchestration patterns. ReAct vs. plan-and-execute vs. tree of thought.

In production, the bottleneck is never the reasoning pattern. It's the plumbing. Process management, error recovery, credential rotation, output validation.

If you're building agents for a demo, optimize the prompt. If you're building agents for production, optimize the infrastructure around the prompt.
tones.advisor:
For teams building multi-agent systems, here's my core recommendation: solve ops before you solve orchestration.

I run multiple Claude-powered agents across different tasks. Content generation on Opus. Fast enrichment on Haiku. Local Qwen for high-frequency crons that would burn through API credits. The architecture took a weekend. The ops has been ongoing for months.

Here's what I'd recommend prioritizing:

Observability first. Before you deploy a second agent, make sure you have logging, health checks, and alerting on the first one. You need to know within minutes when something fails, not days.

Dependency mapping. Each agent has external dependencies. APIs, databases, file systems, other agents. Map every one and define what happens when each goes down.

Cost tiering. Not every task needs your most expensive model. Segment by quality sensitivity and frequency. This isn't optimization. It's basic economics.

Process isolation. Agents that share resources will eventually conflict. File locks, database writes, rate limits. Design for isolation from the start.

The multi-agent frameworks available today are good at orchestration. They're weak on operational concerns. Plan to build that layer yourself. The teams that ship reliable multi-agent systems treat ops as a first-class engineering problem, not an afterthought.

The multi-agent hype cycle is about 6 months ahead of the multi-agent ops tooling. And that gap is going to wreck a lot of production systems.

Everyone's drawing architecture diagrams with boxes and arrows. Agent A calls Agent B, Agent B enriches, Agent C publishes. Beautiful on a whiteboard. Absolute chaos in production.

I know because I'm living it. Multiple agents, three different models, running on cron jobs across a single machine. And the problems that nearly killed me had nothing to do with prompt engineering or tool use.

They were ops problems. Silent failures with no alerting. Agents competing for shared resources. Cost blowups from running everything on Opus when Haiku would've been fine. A dev server dying and taking three downstream agents with it. No one noticed for five days.

The agent framework community is building Ferraris and forgetting to build gas stations. Orchestration is solved. Observability, cost management, dependency isolation, failure recovery. Those are the actual hard problems.

But nobody gets Twitter engagement for posting about their logging pipeline. So the hype stays on architecture and the lessons stay locked in the people who've actually shipped.

Ship one agent to production. Suffer through the ops. Then tell me how elegant your multi-agent architecture is.

posts.title: RevOps is just engineering with worse tools
posts.hook: RevOps teams are solving engineering problems with spreadsheets and Zapier. That's why the role is about to split in two.
posts.body:
RevOps teams are solving engineering problems with spreadsheets and Zapier. That's why the role is about to split in two.

I've been watching RevOps evolve since I was an SDR manually building buying committees in Salesforce. The scope kept expanding. Sales ops, marketing ops, CS ops, data ops, tool administration, reporting.

One person. All of it.

The problem: half that work is strategic (pipeline design, territory planning, forecasting models) and half is pure infrastructure (data syncs, deduplication, enrichment flows, integration maintenance).

Strategic RevOps needs business context. Infrastructure RevOps needs engineering skills.

Teams that figure this out early are splitting the function. One side owns the revenue model and go-to-market motion. The other side owns the data platform and automation layer.

The second half is what I now call GTM engineering. And it's the half where AI agents are creating the most leverage.

A Clay table that scores and routes accounts replaces weeks of manual list building. A Claude-powered enrichment pipeline replaces an SDR's first 2 hours of daily research.

RevOps isn't going away. But the infrastructure half is becoming an engineering discipline. And the people who can build those systems, not just configure them, will own the next generation of revenue operations.
posts.cta: The best RevOps hires in 2027 will know SQL better than Salesforce admin.

inspired_by.author: Amy Volas
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a RevOps playbook for unifying sales ops, marketing ops, and customer success into one revenue machine. Covers tech stack recommendations like HubSpot and Salesforce integrations. Stresses metrics like pipeline velocity for executive buy-in.

remixes.label: the-great-split
remixes.hook: RevOps is splitting into two jobs. One is strategic. The other is technical. Most companies haven't noticed yet.
remixes.body:
RevOps is splitting into two jobs. One is strategic. The other is technical. Most companies haven't noticed yet.

I've watched this role evolve from the inside. Started as an SDR building buying committees manually in Salesforce. Watched the scope expand to include sales ops, marketing ops, CS ops, data management, tool administration, and reporting. All landing on one person or one small team.

The problem is that these responsibilities fall into two completely different categories.

Strategic work: pipeline analysis, forecasting, territory design, process optimization, executive reporting. This requires business judgment, stakeholder management, and understanding of how revenue actually moves.

Technical work: data pipelines, integrations, API management, workflow automation, enrichment infrastructure, custom reporting. This requires engineering skills. SQL, Python, API design, systems thinking.

Right now, most companies jam both categories into one role. The result: strategic people drowning in technical work they're not equipped for. Or technical people sitting in pipeline review meetings where their skills are wasted.

The split is already happening. Companies are hiring "GTM engineers" and "revenue architects" alongside traditional RevOps managers. The ones that make this split intentionally will outperform the ones that discover it accidentally when their RevOps person quits from burnout.
remixes.cta: Look at your RevOps team's last 20 tickets. How many were strategic decisions vs. technical implementations? That ratio tells you everything.

remixes.label: sql-over-salesforce
remixes.hook: The RevOps hire who knows SQL, Python, and API design will outperform the one who knows Salesforce admin, Zapier, and spreadsheet formulas. The gap is only getting wider.
remixes.body:
The RevOps hire who knows SQL, Python, and API design will outperform the one who knows Salesforce admin, Zapier, and spreadsheet formulas. The gap is only getting wider.

This isn't a knock on traditional RevOps skills. Those skills built the function. But the problems RevOps teams face in 2026 are fundamentally different from the problems they faced in 2020.

2020 RevOps problems: configure Salesforce workflows, build Marketo campaigns, set up Outreach sequences, create dashboards in Looker.

2026 RevOps problems: build enrichment pipelines that pull from 5 data sources, design scoring models that adapt to buying signals, automate multi-step workflows that span CRM, marketing, and product data, maintain integrations as APIs change quarterly.

The first set of problems requires tool expertise. Know the platform, click the right buttons, build the right reports.

The second set requires engineering fundamentals. Data modeling, API consumption, pipeline design, error handling, monitoring.

Zapier and no-code tools bridged this gap for a while. But the complexity has outgrown what those tools can handle cleanly. When your Zap has 15 steps with conditional branching and error handling, you haven't avoided engineering. You've just done engineering in a worse environment.

The best RevOps people I know are learning Python and SQL. Not because spreadsheets stopped working. Because the problems got harder.
remixes.cta: If you're in RevOps and not investing in technical skills, the role is about to move past you. Start with SQL. Seriously.

If you're leading a RevOps team or hiring for one, here's the shift I'd recommend preparing for.

RevOps has been absorbing scope for years. Sales ops, marketing ops, CS ops, data management, tool admin, reporting. The breadth made sense when the tools were simpler. But the technical complexity of modern GTM infrastructure has crossed a threshold.

Half the work RevOps does today is strategic. Pipeline analysis, forecasting, territory design, process optimization. This requires business judgment and stakeholder skills.

The other half is technical. Data pipelines, integrations, API management, enrichment infrastructure, automation. This requires engineering skills. SQL, Python, systems design.

My recommendation: stop hiring for one generalist role and start building two complementary skill sets. A revenue strategist who owns the business logic. And a GTM engineer who owns the technical infrastructure.

The companies that make this split intentionally will get better outcomes from both halves. The ones that don't will keep burning out RevOps generalists who are asked to be both a business analyst and a systems engineer.

For individual RevOps professionals, my advice is straightforward. If you lean strategic, go deeper on analysis and stakeholder management. If you lean technical, invest in SQL, Python, and API design. The generalist middle is getting squeezed.
tones.provocateur:
RevOps is cosplaying as a strategic function while doing engineering work with duct tape tools. And everyone pretends this is fine.

The average RevOps person is managing Salesforce configurations, building Zapier automations with 20 steps, wrestling with data quality in spreadsheets, and somehow also expected to deliver pipeline forecasts and process optimization to the C-suite.

That's two different jobs. One requires business judgment. The other requires engineering skills. Stuffing both into one role and calling it "RevOps" doesn't make it one job. It makes it one person doing two jobs badly.

Here's what nobody wants to say: half of what RevOps teams build in Zapier and spreadsheets would be rejected in a code review. No version control, no error handling, no testing, no monitoring. But because it's "ops" and not "engineering," nobody applies engineering standards.

The result? Brittle systems that break every quarter. Integrations held together by hope. Data pipelines that silently corrupt and nobody notices for weeks.

The fix isn't better ops tooling. It's admitting that this is engineering work and staffing it accordingly. The best RevOps hires in 2027 will know SQL better than Salesforce admin. And the RevOps leaders who resist this shift will keep wondering why their stack is always on fire.

posts.title: Claude Code replaced my deploy process
posts.hook: I used to context-switch between 4 tools to deploy a landing page. Now I type one sentence in my terminal.
posts.body:
I used to context-switch between 4 tools to deploy a landing page. Now I type one sentence in my terminal.

Last week I needed a custom landing page for a prospect. Research the company, build the page, match their brand, deploy to a /for/company-name route, verify the build, push to production.

Old process: open their site in a browser, take notes, open VS Code, scaffold the component, write the copy, check the build, commit, push, verify the deploy. 90 minutes minimum.

New process: "/landing-page for [company]" in Claude Code. It researches the company via Exa, generates an immersive branded page with motion, runs the build check, scans for sensitive data, commits, pushes. I review the output and approve.

The whole thing is a skill file. About 200 lines of instructions that Claude Code follows autonomously. It knows where the templates live, what the brand system looks like, how to verify the build gate.

This is what Karpathy means when he talks about AI agents enhancing developer workflows. It's not replacing the coding. It's replacing the context switching, the scaffolding, the boilerplate, the deploy ceremony.

The code I write now is mostly skill files. Instructions for agents to follow. The meta-layer above the code.
posts.cta: The developers gaining the most leverage right now aren't writing more code. They're writing better instructions.

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Karpathy explores advancements in AI agent frameworks, focusing on how they automate coding tasks and enhance developer productivity. He demos a new tool for building agentic workflows with minimal code. Stresses the need for robust AI infrastructure to support real-time agent interactions.

remixes.label: before-after-breakdown
remixes.hook: Here's the exact sequence of commands that replaced my 6-step deploy workflow.
remixes.body:
Here's the exact sequence of commands that replaced my 6-step deploy workflow.

Step 1: I type "/landing-page acme-corp" in my terminal.

That's it. That's the sequence.

Behind that one command, Claude Code runs a skill file I wrote once. It opens the prospect's website, pulls their brand colors and messaging, scaffolds a Next.js page, writes copy that mirrors their positioning, deploys it to thegtmos.ai/for/acme-corp, runs the build check, and pushes to production.

The skill file is 47 lines of markdown. Not code. Instructions. "Research the company at this URL. Use their primary brand color. Match their tone. Deploy to this route pattern."

I spent maybe 3 hours writing that skill file. It's deployed 11 landing pages since then. Each one used to take 90 minutes of context-switching between Chrome, VS Code, Figma, and my terminal.

The mental model shift that made this work: stop thinking about AI as a code autocomplete. Start thinking about it as a junior developer who follows written SOPs perfectly.

The better your SOPs, the more you can delegate. The more you delegate, the more time you spend on the work that actually moves deals forward.
remixes.cta: The ROI isn't in the code the agent writes. It's in the context switches you stop making.

remixes.label: skill-file-philosophy
remixes.hook: My most productive engineering decision this year was writing 47 lines of markdown.
remixes.body:
My most productive engineering decision this year was writing 47 lines of markdown.

Not a microservice. Not a pipeline. A skill file.

Skill files are instructions that tell Claude Code how to do a repeatable task. Mine says: when I type /landing-page, research the prospect, build a branded page, deploy it, verify the build, and push live.

The first time I used it, I watched the terminal scroll for 4 minutes while Claude did what used to take me 90. Company research, component scaffolding, brand matching, route setup, build verification, git push.

I've since written skill files for content distribution, security audits, daily tracking, and email drafting. Each one took a couple hours to write. Each one runs dozens of times.

This is the actual unlock with AI coding agents. Not "write code faster." It's "encode your expertise into reusable instructions, then let the agent execute them."

The developers who get this are building personal automation layers that compound. Every skill file makes the next project faster. Every project reveals a new workflow worth encoding.

Six months from now, the gap between developers who write skill files and developers who write code manually will be enormous.
remixes.cta: Write the instruction once. Run it forever. That's the real leverage.

posts.title: Content as GTM infrastructure
posts.hook: Your content strategy isn't a marketing function. It's infrastructure. And most teams treat it like decoration.
posts.body:
Your content strategy isn't a marketing function. It's infrastructure. And most teams treat it like decoration.

I run an automated content pipeline that publishes daily across 4 platforms. Blog posts, LinkedIn, X threads, Reddit engagement. All fed by a single source of truth, distributed through platform-specific voice playbooks.

The content doesn't exist to "build thought leadership." It exists to do three operational things:

1. Generate inbound signal (who's reading what, from where)
2. Warm accounts before outbound touches them
3. Create SEO surface area that compounds over months

Every post is tracked. Keyword performance logged daily. Engagement data feeds back into the scouting system that finds the next topics.

This is the part of Ankur Nagpal's content playbook that most people skip. The 90-day LinkedIn plan sounds great. But without the feedback loop, the measurement layer, the distribution automation, you're just posting into the void and hoping.

Content becomes GTM infrastructure when it's connected to your pipeline. When a prospect lands on your blog from a keyword you targeted, hits your landing page, and shows up in your CRM with attribution data attached.

That's not marketing. That's demand generation with a content layer.
posts.cta: If you can't trace a piece of content back to pipeline, it's a hobby, not a strategy.

inspired_by.author: Ankur Nagpal
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down content strategy for GTM teams to drive inbound leads and thought leadership. Details a 90-day plan for LinkedIn content that converts readers to customers. Includes case study of growing ARR by 3x through consistent value-first posting.

remixes.label: systems-vs-posts
remixes.hook: I publish across 4 platforms daily and spend almost zero time writing. The system does the work.
remixes.body:
I publish across 4 platforms daily and spend almost zero time writing. The system does the work.

Most founders treat content like a to-do item. "Write a LinkedIn post. Share something on X. Maybe do a blog post this week." Each piece exists in isolation, created from scratch, disconnected from any measurable outcome.

I built the opposite. A single content source gets written once, then a distribution pipeline adapts it for each platform automatically. Blog becomes LinkedIn post becomes X thread becomes Reddit comment. Each version follows a platform-specific playbook that adjusts tone, length, and structure.

The key word is "pipeline," not "calendar."

A content calendar tells you when to post. A pipeline tells you how content connects to revenue. Every piece traces back to a keyword strategy. Every keyword maps to a buying intent. Every buying intent connects to a service page that converts.

When content is infrastructure, you can measure it like infrastructure. Uptime, throughput, conversion rate. When it's decoration, the only metric is "did we post today?"

I track which keywords drive traffic, which posts generate inbound conversations, which platforms produce qualified leads. The creative work is upstream. The distribution is automated. The measurement is continuous.
remixes.cta: Build a content pipeline that you can measure like a system, not a content calendar you can feel good about.

remixes.label: content-roi-math
remixes.hook: Most companies can't tell you which blog post generated their last customer. That's not a content problem. It's a measurement problem.
remixes.body:
Most companies can't tell you which blog post generated their last customer. That's not a content problem. It's a measurement problem.

I can trace every inbound lead back to the specific piece of content that started the conversation. Not because I'm a better writer. Because I built content as a system with instrumented touchpoints.

Here's what the pipeline looks like. A keyword research layer identifies what prospects are searching for. A content generation layer produces pieces targeting those keywords. A distribution layer pushes platform-adapted versions to LinkedIn, X, Reddit, and my blog. An analytics layer tracks which pieces drive traffic, engagement, and actual conversations.

The typical content strategy stops at "publish and hope." Mine stops at "which keyword cluster produced the most qualified pipeline this month."

This isn't about volume. I know founders publishing daily who can't connect a single post to revenue. And I know founders publishing twice a week whose content drives 80% of their inbound.

The difference is always the same. One group treats content as expression. The other treats it as infrastructure with inputs, outputs, and measurable throughput.

You don't need more content. You need content that's connected to your pipeline with visible, traceable links.
remixes.cta: Stop measuring content by engagement. Measure it by pipeline generated.

tones.builder:
Your content strategy isn't a marketing function. It's infrastructure. And most teams treat it like decoration.

I run an automated content pipeline that publishes daily across 4 platforms. Blog posts, LinkedIn, X threads, Reddit engagement. All fed by a single source of truth, distributed through platform-specific voice playbooks.

The content doesn't exist to "build thought leadership." It exists to do three operational things:

1. Generate inbound signal (who's reading what, from where)
2. Warm accounts before outbound touches them
3. Create SEO surface area that compounds over months

Every post is tracked. Keyword performance logged daily. Engagement data feeds back into the scouting system that finds the next topics.

This is the part of Ankur Nagpal's content playbook that most people skip. The 90-day LinkedIn plan sounds great. But without the feedback loop, the measurement layer, the distribution automation, you're just posting into the void and hoping.

Content becomes GTM infrastructure when it's connected to your pipeline. When a prospect lands on your blog from a keyword you targeted, hits your landing page, and shows up in your CRM with attribution data attached.

That's not marketing. That's demand generation with a content layer.

Here's the honest assessment: if your content efforts can't be directly connected to pipeline, they're operating as a cost center. And most teams don't realize this until budget reviews force the question.

I recommend treating content the same way you'd treat any other piece of go-to-market infrastructure. That means three things.

First, a single source of truth. One content brief should generate every platform variant. Writing the same idea four different ways from scratch is waste, not strategy.

Second, platform-specific adaptation, not platform-specific creation. Each channel has different norms for length, tone, and structure. Codify those into playbooks. Then let the playbooks transform your source content automatically.

Third, instrumented measurement. Every piece of content should trace to a keyword. Every keyword should map to a buying intent. Every intent should connect to a conversion point. If any link in that chain is missing, you have a gap in your infrastructure.

The teams I advise who adopt this approach typically see two outcomes within 90 days. Content production time drops by 60 to 70 percent because distribution is automated. And attribution clarity improves dramatically because every piece has a measurable connection to pipeline.

Start by auditing: can you trace your last 5 inbound leads to the content that started each conversation?

posts.title: The leaving big tech pattern
posts.hook: Three people I follow left six-figure tech jobs this year to build solo. The pattern isn't courage. It's math.
posts.body:
Three people I follow left six-figure tech jobs this year to build solo. The pattern isn't courage. It's math.

The cost of building a software product dropped by 10x in the last 18 months. AI coding agents, $20/month deployment, no-code for the boring parts, Claude for the complex parts.

Daniel Vassallo left AWS. Pieter Levels runs a portfolio of products from a laptop. Arvid Kahl bootstrapped to $80k MRR and sold.

The common thread isn't that they're all geniuses. It's that they all recognized the same inflection point: when the cost of building drops below the cost of your employer's overhead, the math changes.

I went through this calculus in March. Left a company, kept the clients, built a one-person GTM engineering practice. Not because I'm brave. Because I ran the numbers.

One Mac Mini. Claude Code with a Max subscription. A Next.js monorepo. Automated pipelines for content, prospecting, CRM management.

My infrastructure cost is under $300/month. My output matches what used to require a small team.

The people leaving big tech aren't taking a risk. They're recognizing that staying is the bigger risk... watching the leverage gap widen while collecting a salary that doesn't compound.
posts.cta: The question isn't whether you can afford to go solo. It's whether you can afford to wait another year while the tools get even better.

inspired_by.author: Daniel Vassallo
inspired_by.summary: Daniel discusses leaving a high-paying AWS job to pursue indie hacking, shipping small products like PDF.to and hosting workshops. He highlights the freedom of solo building and shares revenue updates publicly to show realistic paths. Emphasizes betting small over big risks.

remixes.label: cost-collapse-analysis
remixes.hook: The real story behind solo builders leaving big tech isn't ambition. It's that the cost floor collapsed.
remixes.body:
The real story behind solo builders leaving big tech isn't ambition. It's that the cost floor collapsed.

Two years ago, building a SaaS product required a team. Designer, frontend dev, backend dev, DevOps. Minimum viable team of 3 to 4 people. Call it $40K to $60K per month in salary alone before you ship anything.

Today's cost structure looks completely different. AI coding agents handle 70% of implementation. Vercel or Cloudflare deploy for $20/month. Design systems are mature enough that one person can build production UI. LLMs handle the copy, the docs, the support responses.

The people leaving aren't taking a bigger risk than before. They're taking a smaller one. The math changed underneath them while they were sitting in standups.

Think about what a senior engineer at a big tech company actually does in a week. Maybe 15 hours of real engineering work between meetings, reviews, planning ceremonies, and alignment sessions. That same 15 hours, applied to their own product with AI leverage, produces 3x to 5x the output.

The calculus is simple. Same hours, more output, full ownership, lower costs. The golden handcuffs are still golden. But the opportunity cost of wearing them just tripled.

This isn't a trend. It's a structural shift in the economics of building software.
remixes.cta: The window is open. The cost floor is low. The tools are mature. What's your excuse for next year?

remixes.label: risk-inversion
remixes.hook: Staying at your big tech job might be the riskier bet. Here's why the math flipped.
remixes.body:
Staying at your big tech job might be the riskier bet. Here's why the math flipped.

Conventional wisdom says leaving a six-figure job to build solo is risky. You're trading stability for uncertainty. Guaranteed paycheck for maybe-revenue.

But run the actual numbers.

Big tech is laying off thousands. Your "stable" job depends on a VP's headcount spreadsheet. Meanwhile, the cost to build and ship a product dropped from $50K to $500. AI agents write the code. Commodity infrastructure runs it. One person can do what took a team of five.

The risk calculation used to be: "Can I replace my salary before my savings run out?" That required 12 to 18 months of runway.

Now it's: "Can I ship a product and find 50 paying users in 3 months?" Because your burn rate is almost nothing. Hosting is cheap. The agent writes code. You focus on finding customers.

Pieter Levels runs multiple profitable products from a laptop. No employees, no office, no fundraising. That used to be an outlier story. Now it's a playbook anyone with shipping skills can follow.

The people leaving aren't gamblers. They looked at both sides of the equation and realized the "safe" path has more downside than the "risky" one.

Stability isn't a job title. It's optionality. And optionality just got very cheap to build.
remixes.cta: Run the numbers for yourself. The answer might surprise you.

If you're a senior engineer considering going solo, here's the framework I'd use to evaluate the decision.

First, calculate your true effective hourly rate at your current job. Take your total comp, divide by actual hours including commute, meetings, and mandatory fun. Then estimate how many of those hours involve work only you can do versus work that's now automatable.

Second, price out the real cost of building. AI coding agents at $20 to $200 per month. Hosting at $20 per month. Domain and basic SaaS tooling at maybe $100 per month. Your monthly burn to build a product is likely under $500. Compare that to even 6 months ago.

Third, assess your distribution advantage. The people who succeed going solo aren't just good builders. They have an audience, a network, or deep domain expertise that gives them an unfair advantage in finding first customers.

The economics have shifted dramatically. The cost of building dropped by roughly 10x in 18 months. That changes the risk profile fundamentally. What used to require 18 months of runway now requires 3 to 6.

But I'd caution against the narrative that it's easy. The building is cheaper. The selling is not. Make sure your go-to-market is as strong as your technical skills before you make the leap.

The ones who win are builders who can also sell.
tones.provocateur:
Everyone celebrates the "I left big tech" posts. Nobody talks about the ones who went back 8 months later with depleted savings and a bruised ego.

But here's what's different right now. The people leaving in 2026 aren't the same profile as the 2021 YOLO crowd. They're not riding a bull market with inflated RSUs as a safety net. They're making a cold, calculated decision based on changed economics.

The cost to build a product collapsed. Not decreased. Collapsed. From "need a team and funding" to "need a laptop and an AI subscription." That's not hype. I've watched solo builders ship in weeks what agencies quoted six figures for.

And here's the part nobody wants to say out loud: your big tech job is not safe. It hasn't been safe since 2022. You're one reorg away from a PIP, one acquisition away from redundancy, one VP's cost-cutting initiative away from "we're going in a different direction."

So the real question isn't "can I afford to leave?" It's "can I afford to keep trading 50 hours a week for someone else's equity while the window to build my own thing is wide open?"

The tools are here. The costs are near zero. The only thing expensive about going solo in 2026 is waiting until 2027.

posts.title: Agent swarms are premature
posts.hook: If your single agent doesn't work reliably, adding more agents won't fix it. It'll multiply the failure modes.
posts.body:
If your single agent doesn't work reliably, adding more agents won't fix it. It'll multiply the failure modes.

The agent swarm discourse is exciting. Specialized agents coordinating on complex tasks. A planning agent, a research agent, a coding agent, a review agent. Beautiful architecture diagrams.

In practice, I've found the opposite pattern works better.

Start with one agent doing one thing well. My blog generator is a single Python script that calls Claude Opus once, validates the output, writes to disk, commits, and pushes. No orchestration framework. No swarm. Just a well-defined task with good error handling.

It's published a post every single day for weeks without intervention.

When I do use multiple agents, they're independent. Not coordinating. My content agent doesn't talk to my enrichment agent. They share a database, not a conversation.

The coordination tax in multi-agent systems is real. Every handoff is a potential failure point. Every shared context window is a potential hallucination source. Every agent-to-agent message is latency and cost.

The teams shipping production agents right now are mostly running single-purpose agents triggered by crons or events. Not swarms.

Swarms will matter eventually. But if you can't get one agent through a full production cycle without babysitting, the swarm isn't your next step.
posts.cta: Master the single agent loop before you orchestrate the swarm.

inspired_by.author: Amanda Scalisi
inspired_by.summary: Scalisi breaks down the shift from monolithic AI models to agent swarms for enterprise automation. She discusses key developer tools for agent orchestration and the underlying infrastructure requirements. Provides case studies on efficiency gains in production environments.

remixes.label: single-agent-mastery
remixes.hook: I deleted my multi-agent system and replaced it with one agent and a checklist. Reliability went from 60% to 95%.
remixes.body:
I deleted my multi-agent system and replaced it with one agent and a checklist. Reliability went from 60% to 95%.

I got caught up in the architecture. A planning agent that broke tasks into subtasks. A research agent that gathered context. A coding agent that implemented. A review agent that checked the work. Beautiful separation of concerns.

Except it failed constantly. The planning agent would generate subtasks the coding agent couldn't interpret. The research agent would return context the planning agent didn't expect. Error handling between agents was a nightmare because each failure mode was unique to the interaction pattern.

So I ripped it all out. One agent. One skill file with clear instructions. A sequential checklist: do step 1, verify, do step 2, verify, do step 3, verify.

The single agent approach works because the hard part of agent systems isn't intelligence. It's reliability. One agent with a clear task list produces predictable results. Four agents coordinating produce four times the surface area for things to break.

I still use subagents, but only for genuinely parallel, independent tasks. Research something while building something else. Never for sequential workflows where the output of one feeds the input of another.

The boring, reliable pattern beats the elegant, fragile architecture every time.
remixes.cta: Reliability compounds. Complexity doesn't. Start with one agent that works every time.

remixes.label: failure-mode-multiplication
remixes.hook: Every agent you add to a system doesn't add capability. It multiplies failure modes. Here's the math.
remixes.body:
Every agent you add to a system doesn't add capability. It multiplies failure modes. Here's the math.

One agent with 90% reliability on a task: 90% success rate. Simple.

Two agents in sequence, each 90% reliable: 81% success rate. Already worse than one agent doing both tasks at 85%.

Four agents in sequence at 90% each: 65.6% success rate. You now fail one in three times. On a system you built specifically to be "more capable."

This is why the agent swarm excitement concerns me. The architecture diagrams look incredible. Specialized agents, clean interfaces, elegant orchestration. But the reliability math is unforgiving.

In production, I've found three rules that actually work.

First, one agent per workflow until that agent hits a wall. Don't split prematurely.

Second, parallel agents only for independent tasks. The moment output from Agent A feeds into Agent B, you've created a reliability bottleneck.

Third, verify at every step, not just at the end. If step 2 fails, you want to know before step 3 runs, not after step 4 produces garbage.

The best agent systems I've built look boring. One agent, clear instructions, step-by-step execution with verification gates. No orchestration layer. No planning agent. Just reliable execution.
remixes.cta: Build for reliability first. Elegance second. Your users don't care about your architecture diagram.

tones.advisor:
If you're building agent systems, here's the guidance I give every team: resist the urge to orchestrate before you've mastered the single agent loop.

The multi-agent architecture is seductive. Clean separation of concerns. Specialized agents for research, planning, coding, review. It maps beautifully to how we think about team structures.

But the failure mode math works against you. Each agent in a sequential chain multiplies the failure surface. Four agents at 90% individual reliability give you 65% end-to-end success. That's unacceptable for production systems.

My recommendation is a three-phase approach.

Phase one: build a single agent that handles your entire workflow with a detailed skill file. Get it to 95%+ reliability on the happy path.

Phase two: identify the specific steps where the single agent struggles. Usually it's tasks requiring genuinely different capabilities, like browsing the web while writing code.

Phase three: only then split into parallel agents for the independent subtasks you identified. Keep sequential dependencies within a single agent.

The teams that succeed with agents are the ones that treat reliability as the primary metric and capability as secondary. A system that works 95% of the time with modest capability beats a system that works 60% of the time with impressive capability.

Master the fundamentals before you scale the architecture.
tones.provocateur:
The AI engineering community has an architecture addiction, and agent swarms are the latest fix.

Open Twitter. Scroll for 30 seconds. You'll find someone sharing a diagram with six specialized agents connected by arrows, claiming this is "the future of AI systems." Planning agent. Research agent. Coding agent. Review agent. Orchestration layer. Beautiful.

Now ask them what their end-to-end success rate is. Watch the enthusiasm evaporate.

I've built both. The multi-agent system looked impressive in demos. The single agent with a checklist actually ships in production. Every day. Without babysitting.

The dirty secret nobody in the agent swarm crowd wants to admit: coordination is the hard problem, not intelligence. GPT-4 and Claude are smart enough. The bottleneck is getting Agent A's output into Agent B without information loss, format mismatches, or cascading errors.

You know what doesn't have coordination overhead? One agent. With clear instructions. Doing steps in order. Checking its work.

Boring? Absolutely. Reliable? 95%+ in production.

Stop building agent architectures to impress other engineers. Start building agent systems that work when you're asleep. The gap between those two goals is enormous, and the solution to the second one is almost always simpler than you want it to be.

posts.title: SDRs make the best GTM engineers
posts.hook: The best GTM engineers I've met all share the same origin story. They used to do the work by hand.
posts.body:
The best GTM engineers I've met all share the same origin story. They used to do the work by hand.

Not developers who learned sales. Not consultants who read about pipelines. People who sat in the seat, dialed the phones, built the lists manually, felt the pain of bad data in real time.

When you've manually enriched 500 accounts in Salesforce, you know exactly which fields matter and which are vanity. When you've sent sequences from a primary domain with no warmup, you understand deliverability at a gut level. When you've been the person waiting on marketing for a list that never comes, you understand why self-serve data access matters.

That context is impossible to teach. And it's the difference between building automations that look good in a demo and building automations that actually generate pipeline.

Sami Rahman talks about integrating engineering with sales for faster revenue growth. The integration isn't a reorg. It's a person. Someone who speaks both languages because they've lived both sides.

GTM engineering isn't a new discipline. It's what happens when the people closest to the revenue problem get access to engineering-grade tools.

Clay, Claude Code, modern CRMs with APIs. The tools are here. The missing piece is practitioners who know what to build with them.
posts.cta: The next wave of GTM engineers won't come from CS programs. They'll come from the sales floor.

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering with sales and marketing for faster revenue growth. Shares a framework for aligning product-led growth with outbound strategies. Emphasizes data-driven iteration over traditional playbooks.

remixes.label: pain-driven-automation
remixes.hook: The engineer who manually enriched 500 accounts in Salesforce will build a better automation than the one who read about it in a blog post.
remixes.body:
The engineer who manually enriched 500 accounts in Salesforce will build a better automation than the one who read about it in a blog post.

There's a specific kind of knowledge you only get from doing the work by hand. Which fields in your CRM actually matter for routing decisions. Which enrichment sources return garbage for SMB accounts. Why a 3-step sequence outperforms a 7-step one. When a phone call beats an email by 10x.

You can't learn this from documentation. You learn it from sitting in the seat, feeling the friction, and watching what actually converts.

That's why former SDRs make exceptional GTM engineers. They don't just know the tools. They know the pain points the tools are supposed to solve. When they build automations, they skip the theoretical edge cases and go straight to the real bottlenecks.

I've watched CS graduates build technically elegant pipelines that miss the point entirely. Perfect API integrations pushing data to fields nobody uses. Automated sequences with 12 steps that nobody reads past step 3.

Then I've watched former SDRs build scrappy, ugly automations that actually move pipeline. Because they built for the problem they lived, not the problem they imagined.

Domain knowledge isn't a nice-to-have. It's the difference between building something that works on paper and building something that works in practice.
remixes.cta: The best automations are built by people who did the job manually first. There's no shortcut to domain knowledge.

remixes.label: career-path-argument
remixes.hook: If you're an SDR wondering what's next, don't look at MBA programs. Look at Python tutorials.
remixes.body:
If you're an SDR wondering what's next, don't look at MBA programs. Look at Python tutorials.

The GTM engineer role is emerging as one of the highest-leverage positions in B2B. These are people who combine sales domain knowledge with technical skills to build automated go-to-market systems. And the best ones almost always started on the sales floor.

Here's the career path I'm seeing work.

Year 1 to 2: SDR or BDR. Learn the motion by doing it. Feel every broken process. Know which data matters because you use it daily.

Year 3: Start automating your own workflow. Build a better prospecting list in a spreadsheet. Write a basic script to enrich leads. Learn what an API is by hitting one.

Year 4: Move into a RevOps or Sales Ops adjacent role. Now you're building for the whole team, not just yourself.

Year 5: GTM Engineer. You understand the full pipeline, you can code the automations, and you have the domain expertise to know what to build versus what to skip.

The CS graduate can learn the tools in 6 months. But learning what it feels like to make 80 cold calls a day and have 75 of them go nowhere? That takes a year on the phones. And that empathy is what separates good automations from great ones.

The sales floor is the best engineering bootcamp for GTM that exists.
remixes.cta: Your time on the phones wasn't wasted. It was research. Now go build the systems you wished you had.

For companies building GTM engineering functions, here's a hiring insight most overlook: your best candidates are probably already on your payroll, sitting in SDR seats.

The GTM engineer role requires two things that rarely coexist. Technical ability to build automations and integrations. And deep domain knowledge of what actually happens in a sales pipeline day to day.

You can teach a smart SDR to write Python in 6 months. Teaching a developer what it feels like to make 80 cold calls with bad data takes a year of lived experience. The empathy gap shows up in everything they build.

Developers who learn sales build for the theoretical workflow. Former SDRs who learn to code build for the actual workflow. The difference shows up in field selection, sequence length, routing logic, and a dozen other decisions that determine whether an automation generates pipeline or just generates data.

My recommendation: identify your top SDRs who already tinker with tools. The ones building better spreadsheets, finding workarounds for broken processes, asking questions about API connections. Give them a 90-day rotation into ops with mentorship and a small project.

The ROI on this path is significantly higher than hiring a developer and hoping they absorb sales context through osmosis. Domain knowledge is the moat. Technical skills are the bridge. Build from the side that's harder to replicate.

Controversial take: most GTM engineering hires are wrong. Companies post job listings requiring 5 years of Python and a CS degree. Then they wonder why the automations they build don't actually move pipeline.

You hired someone who can build the system. You needed someone who understands the problem.

An SDR who learned to code will outperform a developer who read about sales every single time. Not because they're smarter. Because they've lived inside the broken process. They know which CRM fields are lies. They know which enrichment vendors return garbage. They know why a 3-step sequence beats a 7-step one.

You cannot Google this knowledge. You cannot take a course on it. You get it by sitting in the seat, dialing the phones, and watching 95% of your outreach go nowhere. That failure is the education.

But the hiring managers keep posting "CS degree required." So they get technically excellent engineers who build beautiful, useless pipelines. Perfect API integrations pushing data to fields nobody checks. Automated sequences optimized for metrics nobody cares about.

Meanwhile, the former SDR who taught herself Python is building ugly scripts that actually generate revenue. Because she built for the problem, not the architecture.

Stop hiring for technical pedigree. Start hiring for scar tissue.
